paragraphs.body: I really hate writing dialogue. I feel stupid writing it, like I’m writing something so juvenile it belongs in a Learn to Read book. I think a lot of it stems from reading bad dialogue. It’s like Lamott says in Bird by Bird, “nothing can break the mood of a piece of writing like bad dialogue.” Sometimes I’ll be reading something really nice, and then, again, as Lamott says, I “hit a patch of dialogue that is so purple and expositional that it reads like something from a childhood play”. I usually avoid it at all costs, especially since I like to write spooky, lyrical, ethereal stuff, and saying “she said” ruins the vibe, and writing “she hissed/whispered/murmured” feels clunky and like Wattpad fanfiction. Up until now, I just haven’t really used it, especially since I tend to learn towards poetry as my chosen writing medium.

annotations.comment: Ha! Yeah, it’s a very frustrating thing to draft — feels more painful, somehow.
annotations.context_right: . I think a lot of i

paragraphs.body: But, slowly, I’m learning how to effectively use dialogue in a way that I’m comfortable with. One of the first stories I’ve read that I really love the way dialogue is written is in Jane Pek’s The Nine-Tailed Fox, from Best American Short Stories 2020. I read this early into the semester because I was drawn in by the folklore and title. It’s spooky and relatively lyrical, just like I like. In the story, there’s a nine-tailed fox, which is a mythical creature from East Asian culture that can turn into a beautiful woman, and she’s narrating her life and past as a demon spirit. The way that Pek writes it, there’s quotation marks around the dialogue of her mortal husband and his friends. And then between the nine-tailed fox and her demon spirit friends, there are no quotations marks. I really loved that idea, to separate through dialogue an immortal spirit creature and regular humans. I also loved how, in this story, Pek gives up information through dialogue that would have been clunky otherwise if tried to convey through actions or thoughts. At the risk of including too long a quote, I really felt like I needed to include this block of text from it. The context is that the nine-tailed fox, and the Pipa, another spirit, are spending a lot of time together when they should not be, and the nine-tailed fox is thinking to herself that this must be what mortals feel like, to feel like they’re losing time.

annotations.comment: Oh, yes, that’s great. I really enjoy how formatting affects the reading experience.

paragraphs.body: “What secret are you and Pipa keeping?, the pheasant asked once. I see you leave every night.
We go on walks, I said. Since we don’t need to sleep, it seems like a waste otherwise.
The pheasant tilted her head and I saw the glowing outlines of her spirit-self in the air above her. Waste of what?
I said, not knowing what else to say, Our time together.
You sound almost like one of them, said the pheasant.”

I was floored by that, honestly. So much happens here in just a few lines—character development, plot development, a reflection on mortality and time. It still feels lyrical and belong in the same tone as the rest of the piece, and because it’s coming from the fox, it’s more personal. Also, the way that I knew who was speaking, even without the “said the pheasant” type thing was really interesting to me. It made me think of Lamott again, in Bird by Bird, writing, “remember that you should always be able to identify each character by what he or she says. Each one must sound different from the others. And they should not all sound like you”. I’m quite bad at making my characters sound distinct from one another and distinct from myself, but I would like to get better at it. I’m learning that not only does dialogue need to be in a form I find misplaced in my style of writing, but also the sheer amount of stuff you can convey through speaking. I want to be more comfortable in finding ways to throw plot development into characters speaking, and I am trying it a little bit.

annotations.context_left: happens here in just a few lines—character development, plot development, a reflection on mortality

paragraphs.body: I haven’t turned it in yet, but in my revised version of Poncho’s Diary, I added in dialogue between Bee and Poncho. I didn’t want to make the dogs talk, because dogs don’t talk. But I did want them to communicate a little bit and I wanted Poncho to learn that he is left alone every day and he always forgets. I thought I’d experiment with the dogs conversing through an action and then a sort of translation. Like, “She lifted her head just enough to attack me with her gaze. Poncho, she was saying, it happens every day.”. I thought I was pretty clever with that, making her talk but not with words. It’s definitely out of my comfort zone, but I’m happy that I’m trying something new. I tried to tie it in with Lamott’s advice, “Sound your words—read them out loud,” to make it not sound clunky. Through the whole dog conversation I was reading it out loud and trying to think about what the dogs said.

In the same vein of writing dialogue in a non traditional way, I think a lot about both The Semplica-Girl Diaries by George Saunders and also Dante Samarco’s story A Protagonist to Protagonist. Both stories write dialogue in a more script form. For Semplica-Girl, Saunders writes it this way because he’s writing in a diary, so it makes sense in the sort of found form we’re reading (“Lilly: Wow, this garden is like ten times bigger than out whole yard”). And in A Protagonist to Protagonist, Samarco write it like a movie script. While the form didn’t feel like it fit so well in that story, it was still a really interesting way to including dialogue. I’m learning that there are so many ways dialogue can appear on the page and how much that can influence tone.

There is also, of course, just writing dialogue the normal way, but making it fit well into a story. This is the one I’m most hesitant of, because it’s so hard for me to use the subscripts like “he said/she said” and to make it fit nicely following or proceeding an action or thought. I think Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie did a really nice job of that in Cell One. It wasn’t out of the ordinary the way Adichie made her characters talk, and sometimes it just helped make a scene fuller (like in church when Nnambia leaves the narrator alone and the mass ends with the Priest saying, “The Mass is ended, go in peace”).  Sometimes it reveals information, like when Nnamabia tells the narrator about why he was transferred prisons. There’s no way she could have known that unless he said it, and that simple exchange made the details so much more real.

annotations.comment: Agree. It also has the feel of remembered dialogue — how we can often remember the exact words someone used (for some reason!).

paragraphs.body: In my first short story, I didn’t really use dialogue at all, because the only speaking lines were the lyrics from the song it was based on, which didn’t fit or flow well at all into my story and I am actively revising it to be better. I think in that story, the two girls will just speak, and I will write the dialogue normally, and it will give exposition into why the one girl is buried and why the other is digging. I have so many ideas, like this is a cycle that happened more than once, and I think the best way to convey that is to learn it while the girls learn it through them speaking. I want to try Lamott’s tips of speaking it out loud to make sure it isn’t stupid, and to, as she says, “encompass what is said and what is not said”. Once I know what they need to say, I need to figure out how to make them say it. Part of me feels like “she said” is too boring for a poetic thing, but “she whispered” just makes me cringe. But the readings we’ve done have shown me that it’s okay to just say “she said”, so I’ll probably do that.

annotations.comment: I think what happens when it’s this simple is that it just disappears to the reader. We don’t even really notice it.

paragraphs.body: I’m realizing that dialogue doesn’t have to fight against the tone I want and it can even work with it. There’s so much potential in what characters say—how they say it, when they don’t, and what gets revealed otherwise. I’m definitely going to use it more going forward.

annotations.comment: Lovely. It’s been a delight having you in class, Sophia, and this wonderful essay is another good example of why. Wishing you all the best!
